data.name: Soft Japanese Coffee Jelly With Salted Caramel Cream
data.description: A delicately set, bittersweet Japanese-style coffee jelly that melts instantly on the tongue, crowned with a rich, freshly whipped salted caramel cream for the perfect post-dinner pick-me-up.

refrigerator.notes: Keep glasses tightly covered with plastic wrap to prevent the jelly from absorbing refrigerator odors. Add the whipped cream just before serving.

freezer.notes: Freezing will destroy the delicate gelatin structure and cause weeping upon thawing.

instructions.text: Place the cold water in a small bowl and sprinkle the unflavored gelatin powder evenly over the surface. Let it sit undisturbed for 5 minutes to bloom and fully hydrate.

doneness_cues.visual: Gelatin looks like a solid, spongy mass with no dry powder visible

instructions.text: In a saucepan, combine the hot water, espresso powder, and granulated sugar. Heat gently over medium heat, stirring occasionally, until the sugar and coffee dissolve completely. The temperature should reach about 60°C/140°F. Do not let it boil.
Remove the saucepan from the heat. Add the bloomed gelatin mass to the hot coffee mixture and whisk thoroughly until the gelatin is completely dissolved and no lumps remain.

doneness_cues.visual: Liquid is perfectly smooth and translucent when poured off a spoon

instructions.text: Divide the coffee mixture evenly among 4 serving glasses. Allow to cool briefly at room temperature, then cover tightly with plastic wrap and refrigerate for at least 4 hours until gently set.

doneness_cues.tactile: Jelly gently jiggles when the glass is tapped but holds its shape

instructions.text: Just before serving, pour the cold heavy cream into a mixing bowl and add the chilled salted caramel sauce. Using a hand mixer or whisk, beat until soft peaks form.

doneness_cues.visual: Cream holds its shape lazily but flops over at the tip when the whisk is lifted

tips: Ensure the heavy cream is straight from the refrigerator. Cold cream whips faster and holds a more stable structure.

instructions.text: Spoon the freshly whipped salted caramel cream generously over the chilled coffee jelly. Garnish with a tiny pinch of flaky sea salt if desired, and serve immediately.

troubleshooting.symptom: Jelly does not set properly and remains a thick liquid
troubleshooting.likely_cause: The gelatin was boiled, which destroys its protein structure and setting properties, or the jelly was not chilled long enough.
troubleshooting.prevention: Always remove the base liquid from the heat before adding bloomed gelatin, and ensure a minimum 4 hours of chilling time.
troubleshooting.fix: Cannot be fixed if boiled. If simply under-chilled, return to the refrigerator for another 2 to 4 hours.

troubleshooting.symptom: Gelatin clumps in the coffee mixture
troubleshooting.likely_cause: Gelatin was poured into water instead of sprinkled over it, or it was not fully bloomed before adding to the hot liquid.
troubleshooting.prevention: Always sprinkle gelatin powder evenly over the surface of cold water to allow even hydration.
troubleshooting.fix: Pass the warm coffee mixture through a fine mesh strainer to remove gelatin lumps before chilling.

chef_notes: For the softest, signature melt-in-the-mouth texture of Japanese coffee jelly, the ratio of gelatin to liquid is crucial. This recipe uses slightly less gelatin than standard Western jellies to achieve that delicate tremble.
If you prefer to use freshly brewed coffee instead of espresso powder and water, simply substitute 445ml of strong, hot pour-over or drip coffee in place of the hot water and espresso powder.
Adjust the sugar in the coffee base to your preference. Traditional Japanese coffee jelly leans heavily toward the bitter side to contrast with the rich, sweet cream on top.
data.cultural_context: Coffee jelly (kohī zerī) was popularized in Japanese coffee shops known as kissaten during the Taisho period and saw a massive boom in the 1960s. It represents the distinct Japanese culinary ethos of taking a Western ingredient and adapting it into a delicately balanced, texturally refined local favorite.
